name: Italian Cuisine
description: Pasta, pizza, regional specialties, and the traditions of la cucina italiana.

questions.q: Which Italian city is the birthplace of the modern Margherita pizza?

source.notes: Made by Raffaele Esposito in 1889 for Queen Margherita.

questions.q: What three colors of the Italian flag are represented on a Margherita pizza?
Which long-grain rice variety is most commonly used in risotto?

source.notes: Carnaroli and Vialone Nano are also acceptable risotto rices.

questions.q: Which Italian city is most associated with risotto, particularly the saffron-flavored 'risotto alla Milanese'?
What is 'al dente' pasta?
choices: Firm to the bite — slight resistance when chewed

questions.q: Which thin, dry-cured Italian ham is traditionally served as antipasto with melon?

source.notes: Prosciutto di Parma is the most famous PDO version.

questions.q: What pork product cured with pork-cheek is the traditional base of Sugo all'Amatriciana?
Which Italian region is the home of Parmigiano-Reggiano cheese?

source.notes: PDO production limited to Parma, Reggio Emilia, Modena and parts of Mantua and Bologna.

questions.q: Which Italian region is the birthplace of Bolognese (ragù alla bolognese) sauce?

source.notes: Officially registered with the Bologna Chamber of Commerce in 1982.

questions.q: What is the proper Italian name for what Americans call 'Spaghetti Bolognese'?

source.notes: Bolognese is paired with tagliatelle traditionally; spaghetti is an Anglo-American adaptation.

questions.q: Which classic pasta dish features eggs, guanciale, pecorino, and black pepper — but NO cream?

source.notes: Cream is a non-Italian addition — true carbonara uses eggs to coat the pasta.

questions.q: What two simple ingredients give 'cacio e pepe' its name?

source.notes: Pecorino Romano + black pepper, with starchy pasta water.

questions.q: Which narrow, flat-stranded Italian pasta takes its name from the Italian for 'little tongues'?

source.notes: audit-corrected 2026-05-09: replaced 'ribbon-shaped' phrasing (better fits tagliatelle/fettuccine) with 'narrow, flat-stranded'. Linguine = 'little tongues'.

questions.q: What is the Italian feast tradition celebrated on Christmas Eve, named after a number of seafood courses?

source.notes: More common in Italian-American families than in Italy itself.

questions.q: Which Italian region is famous for thin, crispy focaccia bread topped with olive oil and rosemary?

source.notes: Focaccia di Recco and focaccia genovese are the famous styles.

questions.q: Which Italian island is the home of cannoli, arancini, and granita?
What Italian term means 'before the meal' — referring to pre-meal small bites?

source.notes: Aperitivo is the pre-dinner drink ritual.

questions.q: Which Italian sparkling wine, made in Veneto, has surpassed Champagne in global sales?
What Italian dessert means 'pick-me-up' and contains coffee, mascarpone, and ladyfingers?

source.notes: Tiramisù literally means 'pick me up' (the caffeine boost).

questions.q: How does Italian gelato differ from American ice cream?
choices: Less butterfat and less air (denser texture)

source.notes: Gelato uses more milk than cream and is churned slowly.
